commentary: Wrapped five songs of the demo EP at Copperline this week. DT and Terrell made the room feel easy. Grateful for this crew.
Cut a new co-write with the Silverbell crew today. Three verses and a bridge before lunch. Some days the song just shows up.
Sang harmonies on a session this morning. Nothing like stacking vocals till the track lifts off the floor.
Throwback to learning Patsy Cline records off my abuela's old radio in San Antonio. Still chasing that feeling.
New hold from a Silverbell pitch this week. Fingers crossed it finds the right artist.
Tracking week at Copperline. Coffee, scratch vocals, and a whole lot of takes.
Sunday morning with the New Harvest choir. Singing for the joy of it resets everything.
Demo day. Freddy Fender and Selena on the drive in. You can hear Texas in everything I write.
Honored to have one of my songs cut. Hearing your words in someone else's voice never gets old.
Late-night writing session. Sometimes the best lines come after midnight.
Spent the afternoon on vocal rest and steam. Taking care of the instrument so it lasts.
Co-wrote with a new collaborator today. Two strangers, one guitar, and a song by sundown.
Pulled an old conjunto record out and got lost in Flaco Jimenez's accordion. Roots run deep.
Five-song demo is mixed and ready to pitch. Proud of this batch with DT and Terrell.
Session singer life: in by nine, three songs cut, home for supper.
Writing round downtown tonight. Love hearing what everybody's been working on.
Grateful for the folks at Silverbell who believe in these songs.
Found a pair of vintage cowboy boots that needed a good home. Stage-ready.
Tracking vocals at Copperline with Terrell at the board. The man hears everything.
Started a new song this morning. Just a melody and a feeling so far, but it's a start.
Two weeks of tracking behind me and the demo finally sounds like the thing I heard in my head. Thank you to the crew at Copperline for keeping the coffee hot and the takes honest.
A hold on one of my songs went cold this week. You learn to hold these things loosely and start the next one the same afternoon.
Spent the morning stacking three-part harmony on a friend's single. Session singing is the quiet craft that keeps my lights on, and I love it.
I grew up learning Selena and Freddy Fender records off my Abuelita's kitchen radio in San Antonio. Everything I write still bends back toward that sound.
Vanessa walked two of my songs into a Music Row room this week. However it lands, being in the running is the honor.
Split sheets before anyone leaves the room. I've said it a hundred times and I'll keep saying it until it's second nature for every writer I work with.
The best songwriter rounds in this town are the ones nobody is live-streaming. Show up early, listen hard, and take the notes.
Six months of daily vocal warmups after a scare with my cords taught me the voice is an instrument you maintain, not one you just use.
Helped my cousin Reggie build a lyric-and-chord unit for his high school music class back home in San Antonio. Proud of what those kids are writing.
Quarterly review with Silverbell today: holds, cuts, and royalty statements. The unglamorous half of a creative life, and the half that keeps it going.
DT reworked a bridge on track two and suddenly the whole song made sense. A good producer hears the version you haven't written yet.
Wrote a verse about my dad this week for the first time. Almost twenty years, and the song finally let me say it out loud.
Cut scratch vocals on all five demo tracks in one long day. Tired in the best way. This EP is starting to feel real.
If you can blend from a mariachi belt to a breathy country phrase in a single take, you will always have session work in this town.
Sunday morning with the New Harvest choir. Forty voices, no auto-tune, and a reminder of why I ever opened my mouth to sing.
Pitched a co-write to a supervisor for a small indie film. Sync is a long game, but one placement can change a year.
Terrell's rough mix made me tear up in the booth. When the engineer serves the song instead of the spectacle, everybody wins.
Started a new song this morning with just a melody and a feeling. That is usually the hardest and the most hopeful part.
Learned early that a hook you can hum on the drive home is worth more than a clever line you have to explain.
Found a pair of 1970s Western boots at an estate sale in Franklin and broke them in walking to the studio. Stage-ready and settled law.
Co-wrote today with a writer Vanessa introduced me to. She hears harmony the way I do, which is rarer than you'd think.
Backup vocal day. You will not see me in the video, but you'll hear me holding the third above the lead. That is the job.
Registered a batch of new songs with my PRO this week. Protect the paperwork with the same care you give the chorus.
Pozole on the stove and a legal pad on the counter. Some songs get finished in the kitchen, not the writing room.
Grateful to the folks at Silverbell who keep believing in these songs even when the pitches come back quiet.
A writing round at the Riverstone tonight. Four writers, four chairs, one mic. This is the version of Nashville I moved here for.
Tracked harmonies over a demo for a first-time artist today. Watching someone hear their own song come alive never gets old.
Pulled an old conjunto record and lost an hour to Flaco Jimenez's accordion. My roots run through everything I put to tape.
The demo budget is my own money, and every dollar sharpens the decisions. Belief has to outlast the bank balance.
Reworked a chorus that had been fighting me for a month. Sometimes the fix is one word you were too stubborn to cut.
Radnor Lake before the day heats up. Songwriting walks clear more blocks than any amount of staring at the page.
Sang on a session with a drummer I'd never met, and by the third take we were finishing each other's phrases. Nashville magic.
A hold turned into a pass this week, and a different song I'd forgotten got picked up. You never know which one it'll be.
Cafe de olla, a blank page, and no meetings until noon. The best mornings I know.
Talked a young writer through their first split sheet today. Passing on what someone once patiently taught me.
Mixed track three at Copperline until it stopped sounding like words and started sounding like a feeling. That's when you know.
Homesick for San Antonio this week, so I built the setlist around the songs my family raised me on.
A supervisor asked for comps in a Tejano-country lane that country radio hasn't quite figured out yet. Happy to be early.
Vocal rest, steam, and honey today. Boring maintenance, but the instrument thanks you for it later.
Watched Carly Thornton's single climb the country chart with a song I helped write. Heard it in a gas station over the holidays and just stood there.
Two thirds of a song and a hook I can't stop humming. Left the co-write already planning the next session.
The demo EP is mixed and ready to pitch. Five songs, a lot of late nights, and a crew I trust with my life.
Session work taught me to sing for the song and not for the mirror. The lead gets the spotlight; I get the blend.
Spent the afternoon voicing a melody against a new chord shape. Small experiments are how the sound stays yours.
Grateful for a producer in DT who paces the room while I sing the same line nineteen times and never once rushes me.
Wrote something honest enough that I'm a little scared to play it. In my experience that fear means it's real.
A friend asked how I handle the rejections. Honestly, I finish the coffee, then I write the next one.
Nashville networking, the real kind: I met my best collaborator standing in line at a songwriter round, not at a conference.
Cut a demo vocal for a ballad this morning and a two-step this afternoon. Range is what keeps the calendar full.
Building a Dia de los Muertos setlist for a small show. The old songs carry the people we've lost, so I sing them carefully.
Learned to leave space in a vocal take. The rest between phrases is where the listener catches their breath with you.
A cowriter and I finally cracked the second verse that had stalled us for weeks. Persistence is most of this craft.
Heartland Credit Union, an Uber to the studio, and a guitar case. That's the whole operation some days, and it's enough.
Sat in on a mix review and mostly kept quiet. Knowing when not to add a harmony is its own skill.
Pitched three songs this month, got one hold, and wrote four new ones. The math of a working songwriter.
Keisha and I traded harmony ideas over the phone for an hour tonight. The best friendships in this town are also the best rooms.
A young artist thanked me for the harmony I laid on their record. That message meant more than any chart position.
Coffee with Vanessa to talk pitch strategy for the winter. Publishing is patience wearing a nice jacket.
Reworked a lyric so it sang better even though it read worse on paper. Songs are for the ear, not the eye.
Tracked at Magnolia Sound for a change of room today. A new space can shake a stubborn arrangement loose.
Taught myself a new picking pattern this week just to keep the hands honest. Growth beats comfort every time.
The choir carried the descant Sunday and I just listened. Some weeks the best thing a singer can do is receive.
A pitch note came back with real feedback instead of silence. In this business, a specific no is a gift.
Cut a bilingual verse into a country song today and it felt like coming home. San Antonio in a Nashville room.
Spent the morning organizing voice memos. Memo 412 became a hold once; you keep them all because you never know.
Sang on a session for scale and left with two new friends and one new idea. Not a bad day's work.
Learned the hard way to date and label every demo. Future you will thank present you when the cut request comes.
A slow week for pitches, a good week for writing. The garden grows whether or not anyone is watching yet.
Rehearsed with the band for an upcoming round. Tightening the harmonies until they lock is my favorite kind of work.
A supervisor placed a friend's song in a national spot this week. Watching your peers win feeds your own belief.
Wrote a chorus in the Uber home and voice-memoed it at a red light. The songs arrive on their own schedule.
Talked with a first-time writer about handling co-writes with grace. Leave your ego at the door and the song gets better.
Steam, warmups, then four hours of harmony passes. The routine is boring and the routine is everything.
Cut a demo for a two-step that I hope some dance floor gets to wear out someday. That's the whole dream, honestly.
A quiet win: a song I wrote two years ago finally found its artist. Patience isn't passive in this line of work.
Sat with an arrangement that wasn't working and finally admitted the key was wrong. Ego is the last thing to leave the room.
Recorded a rough guide vocal at midnight because the feeling wouldn't wait until morning. Sometimes it won't.
Grateful for the session players in this town who make a scratch track sound like a record on the first pass.
Reviewed royalty statements over coffee and reminded myself the pennies from streams still add up to rent.
Wrote toward a title I'd been carrying for months and the song finally caught up to it. Titles can lead you home.
A round tonight, a session tomorrow, church on Sunday. The rhythm of a working week I'm thankful for.
Cut the last harmony on the last demo track today. Five songs done, and now the real work of pitching begins.
Learned more about phrasing from one afternoon behind the glass than from a year of trying to be clever.
Closing out a long writing stretch grateful for my family, my crew, and a city that still makes room for a slow song.
Spent Saturday teaching my niece and nephew the harmony to an old family song over FaceTime. Passing the melody down is its own kind of publishing.
